title: Ibongia t/a Quantech Consultancy v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes (Tax Appeal
text:
Ibongia t/a Quantech Consultancy v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes (Tax Appeal
E713 of 2025) [2026] KETAT 81 (KLR) (Appeals) (27 March 2026) (Judgment)
Neutral citation: [2026] KETAT 81 (KLR)
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEAL TRIBUNAL
APPEALS
TAX APPEAL E713 OF 2025
RO OLUOCH, CHAIR, C WESONGA & E KOMOLO, MEMBERS
MARCH 27, 2026
BETWEEN
INNOCENT MOMANYI IBONGIA T/A QUANTECH
CONSULTANCY ...................................................................................... APPELLANT
AND
COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES ...................................... RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
1. The Appellant is a tax resident in Kenya registered under Personal Identication Number
A001189327R.
2. The Respondent is the principal ocer appointed under section 13 of the Kenya Revenue Authority
Act. The Kenya Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government of Kenya mandated with the duty
of collecting and receipting all tax revenue, and the administration and enforcement of all tax laws
set out in Parts 1 & 2 of the First Schedule to the Act, for the purposes of assessing, collecting, and
accounting for all tax revenues in accordance with those laws.
3. On 28th January 2025, the Respondent issued the Appellant a tax demand of Ksh. 30,650,015
(inclusive of interest and penalties) for Value Added Tax (VAT) and Income Tax for the years 2020
to 2022.
4. The Appellant, vide a letter dated 27th March 2025 lodged an Objection against the said assessment.
5. On 23rd May 2025, the Respondent issued the Appellant an Objection Decision conrming the tax
demand of Kshs. 30,650,015.
6. Appellant, being dissatised with the Respondent’s Objection Decision, lodged this Appeal vide
Notice of Appeal dated and led on 20th June 2025.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 1

The Appeal
7. In its Memorandum of Appeal dated 3rd July 2025 and led on 4th July 2025, the Appellant raised the
following grounds of appeal: -
a. That the Respondent erred in law and fact in disallowing operating expenses incurred by the
Appellant in relation to purchases, direct wages, legal fees, repairs, telephone charges, traveling
costs, and insurance, yet the Appellant had incurred these expenses wholly and exclusively for
the purpose of ensuring the generation of income and in accordance with Section 15(1) of the
Income Tax Act; hence, were allowable expenses.
b. That the Respondent erred in law and fact in disallowing the input claimed by the taxpayer on
the grounds that the inputs claimed were non-allowable as provided in Section 17(1) and 17(4)
of the VAT Act 2013. However, the input VAT had been correctly claimed for vatable supplies
purchased by the Appellant and within six months in which the company was permitted to
deduct input VAT as per the provisions of Section 17(2) of the VAT Act 2013.
c. That in view of the foregoing, the Appellant is apprehensive that the actions of the Respondent
lack merit, are unlawful and gravely unjust, and that unless the orders sought are granted, the
Appellant risks being unjustly required to pay for the alleged taxes to its prejudice.
Appellant’s Case
8. The Appellant case is based on its Statement of Facts dated 3rd July 2025 and led on 4th July, 2025,
and its Written Submissions dated 25th February, 2026.
9. The Appellant averred that several operating expenses had been classied as non-deductible without
proper justication; yet, they were necessary for the day to day running of the business and therefore
qualify as deductible expenses under Section 15 of the Income Tax Act.
10. The Appellant relied on Section 15 of the Income Tax Act, which states as follows:
“15. Deductions allowed (1) For the purpose of ascertaining the total income of any
person for a year of income, there shall, subject to section 16 of this Act, be
deducted all expenditure incurred in such year of income which is expenditure
wholly and exclusively incurred by him in the production of that income, and
where under section 27 of this Act any income of an accounting period ending
on someday other than the last day of such year of income is, for the purpose
of ascertaining total income for any year of income, taken to be income for any
year of income, then such expenditure incurred during such period shall be
treated as having been incurred during such year of income.”
11. The Appellant stated that reclassifying these expenses undermined their role in the production of
income and unfairly inated its tax liability.
12. That the Respondent was unjustied in disallowing the said deductible expenses and thereafter
demanding corporate taxes attributable to the additional income due to the disallowed amounts for
the period under review.
13. The Appellant further submitted that the Respondent disallowed inputs claimed by it on the grounds
that the inputs claimed were non-allowable as provided in Section 17(4) of the VAT Act 2013.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 2

14. The Appellant averred that, based on the list of the disallowed inputs, the items therein related to the
Taxpayer’s ordinary course of business and qualied for VAT input claims.
15. The Appellant concluded by stating that the issuance of the Tax Demand dated 28th January 2023 and
the subsequent Objection Decision of 23rd May 2025 by the Respondent is without basis and illegal.
16. In its Written Submissions dated 25th February 2026, the Appellant reiterated the above assertions.
Appellant’ Prayers
17. The Appellant prayed to the Tribunal for the following orders: -
a. That it vacates the tax assessment and Objection Decision by the Respondent dated 23rd May
2025 of Kshs. 30,650,015.
b. Costs to the Appellant.
Respondent’s Case
18. The Respondent led its Statement of Facts dated 10th July 2025 and led on 29th August 2025 in
opposition to the Appeal. The Respondent also led its Written Submissions dated 2nd March 2026.
19. The Respondent averred that the assessment arose as a result of a return review exercise on the
Appellant for the period 2020-2023 following perpetual business losses from the Appellant’s business
and credit VAT returns attributable to huge expense claims.
20. That on 28th January 2025, the Respondent issued the Appellant additional income tax assessments
and VAT assessments as detailed below:
Tax Obligation Principal Penalty Interest Total
Income Tax - 17,269,698 928,201 6,844,652 25,042,551
Company
VAT 3,094,521 409,471 2,103,473 5,607,465
Total 20,364,219 1,337,672 8,948,125 30,650,016
21. The Respondent stated that an analysis of the Appellant’s individual tax returns revealed that it had
claimed withholding tax credits from UNES without declaring the respective business income for the
period under review.
22. That the expenses claimed by the Appellant were not attributable to business income but to Monthly
Rental Income, which the Respondent went ahead and disallowed as per the table below:
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 3

Period Principal Interest Penalty Total
2020 11,713,930 5,200,362 609,492 17,523,784
2021 4,230,788 1,392,544 252,460 5,875,792
2022 1,324,980 251,746 66,249 1,642,975
Total 17,269,698 6,844,652 928,201 25,042,551
23. The Respondent further stated that the Appellant’s business income received from consultancy
practice in the form of fees, as well as corresponding expenses incurred in furtherance of the business,
ordinarily attracts VAT. That however, an analysis of the Appellant’s records indicates that the
Appellant made input claims on expenses related to Monthly Rental Income, including building
materials, cement, ttings, cornices, electrical items, and various other items.
24. The Respondent submitted that upon review of the Appellant’s AFS and IT2C returns, it was noted
that the Appellant had claimed costs of sales which comprised building materials, building works, and
wages, as well as operating expenses.
25. The Respondent argued that given the Appellant’s principal business activity is that of a quantity
surveyor, it was not clear how these costs were incurred to generate income from consultancy fees and
yet seemingly related to construction/rental income.
26. The Respondent relied on Section 6A of the Income Tax Act, which provides for the imposition of
residential rental income tax.
27. The Respondent contended that property owners with rental income above Kshs. 15,000,000 are
required to declare the rental income together with incomes from other sources (if any) while ling
their annual income tax returns.
28. The Respondent stated that the Appellant, in their audited nancial statements, declared income from
consultancy fees and declared rental income under other income.
29. However, the Appellant led his Monthly Rental Income, which is a nal tax for the period under
review, and did not provide any evidence of a notice in writing to the Commissioner not to be taxable
under MRI as provided in law.
30. The Respondent argued that the Appellant’s assertions that the expenses in question were incurred
for income generation have not been supported, and as such, the taxes as assessed were conrmed.
31. The Respondent further averred that a review of the Appellant’s VAT returns showed that the
Appellant had claimed building materials, car parts, cement, cornice, and entertainment for the period
under review. The Respondent, however, noted that the Appellant claimed input tax on car parts and
entertainment, which are prohibited as per the provisions of Section 17(4) of the VAT Act.
32. The Respondent contended that despite the Appellant’s assertions that the expenses were incurred
for income generation, he has been unable to demonstrate how they were incurred to make taxable
supplies, and as such, the taxes as assessed remained due and payable.
33. The Respondent stated that, in light of the above, the Objection was fully rejected and the resultant
tax of Kshs. 31,887,248 inclusive of penalties and interest, conrmed.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 4

34. The Respondent reiterated that the Appellant had failed to discharge its burden of proof in proving
that the Respondent’s tax decision is incorrect as per the provisions of Section 56(1) of the Tax
Procedures Act.
35. The Respondent relied on the ruling of the High Court in the case of Commissioner of Investigations
& Enforcements vs. Evans Kidero (2022) eKLR where the duty of the taxpayer to keep proper records
was emphasized.
36. The Respondent reiterated its averments in its Written Submissions dated 2nd March 2026.
Respondent’s Prayers
37. The Respondent prayed to the Tribunal for the following orders:
a. The Appeal be dismissed in its entirety.
b. The Respondent’s Objection Decision dated 23rd May, 2025 be upheld.
c. Costs of the Appeal be awarded to the Respondent.
Issues For Determination
38. The Tribunal, having considered the parties' pleadings, submissions, and documents led before it, is
of the view that the following issue falls for its determination: whether the Respondent’s Objection
Decision dated 23rd May, 2025 is justied.
Analysis And Determination.
39. This Appeal was triggered by the Respondent’s Objection Decision dated 23rd May, 2025, which
conrmed additional income tax assessments of the Appellant amounting to Kshs. 30,650,015
inclusive of penalty and interest. The Respondent justied its Objection Decision on the grounds that
the Appellant did not provide sucient documents and explanations to support its objection against
the additional assessments.
40. The Tribunal noted that in its pleadings and submissions, the Respondent armed that the Appellant
failed to materially support its assertions or rebut the ndings in the Statement of Findings as laid
out in the Objection Decision. That specically for Income Tax, the Respondent stated that a review
of the Appellant’s Audited Financial Statements and its IT2C returns showed that the cost of sales,
as claimed by the Appellant, represented building materials, building works, and wages, as well as
related operating expenses. The Respondent questioned the applicability of these costs to the principal
business activity of the Appellant, Quantity Surveyor Consultancy, whose income should primarily
be Consultancy Fees.
41. The Tribunal equally noted that the Respondent further questioned the Appellant’s classication
of rental income, where the Appellant, in its audited nancial statements, declared income from
consultancy fees and declared rental income under other incomes and went ahead to claim
corresponding expenses. However, the Respondent observed that the Appellant still led its Monthly
Rental Income (MRI), which is a nal tax, and there was no evidence of a notice in writing to the
Commissioner not to be taxable under MRI, as provided above.
42. The Tribunal further noted that in its Statement of Facts and submissions, the Appellant asserted that
it wholly and exclusively incurred deductible business expenses within the meaning of Section 15 of
the Income Tax Act, which the Respondent disallowed and added back to income.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 5

43. Arising from the above rival positions and the Objection Decision dated 23rd May 2025, the Tribunal
is of the considered view that the primary issue in contention is whether the Appellant suciently
supported its objection to the Respondent’s additional assessment, which could have led to a variation
of its tax liability.
44. From the Objection Decision and the record before the Tribunal, it is not contested that the
Respondent specically addressed the treatment of Monthly Rental Income and its corresponding
expenses and tasked the Appellant to elaborate on its declaration of monthly rental income.
45. Section 59 of the Tax Procedures Act (Cap 469B) provides as follows regarding the Appellant’s duty to
produce documents and records as may be sought by the Respondent: -
1. For the purposes of obtaining full information in respect of the tax liability of any person
or class of persons, or for any other purposes relating to a tax law, the Commissioner or an
authorized ocer may require any person, by notice in writing, to –
a. produce for examination, at such time and place as may be specied in the notice, any
documents (including in electronic format) that are in the person's custody or under
the person's control relating to the tax liability of any person;
b. furnish information relating to the tax liability of any person in the manner and by the
time specied in the notice; or
c. attend, at the time and place specied in the notice, for the purpose of giving evidence
in respect of any matter or transaction appearing to be relevant to the tax liability of
any person.
46. Section 56 (1) of the Tax Procedures Act (cap 469B) provides as follows regarding the Appellant’s
burden of proof: -
In any proceedings under this Part, the burden shall be on the taxpayer to prove that a tax
decision is incorrect.
47. Furthermore, Section 30 of the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act (cap 469A) provides as follows on the
Appellant’s burden of proof: -
In a proceeding before the Tribunal, the appellant has the burden of proving (a)where an
appeal relates to an assessment, that the assessment is excessive; or (b)in any other case, that
the tax decision should not have been made or should have been made dierently.
48. The Tribunal notes that the Appellant did not specically address themselves to shedding light on
the issues raised by the Respondent in the Objection Decision. The Tribunal has not sighted any
documents presented by the Appellant that would clarify whether the Appellant indeed declared
its rental income under the simplied tax of the MRI or whether it sought permission from the
Commissioner to be excluded from the same and declare this income as part of the other income of
its business.
49. The Tribunal has perused the provisions that guide the exclusion of MRI as dictated in the Income Tax
Act, Residential Income Tax Regulations under Legal Notice 106 of 2016 where the following is stated:
“4. Election to be excluded from residential rental income tax
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 6

(1)A person who opts not to be subject to the residential rental income tax
under section 6A of the Act shall notify the Commissioner at least three
months before the end of the year of income.
5. Residential rental income tax as a nal tax
Any income from rent that is subject to residential rental income tax shall not
be liable to any other tax under the Act.
6. Records
A person subject to residential rental income tax shall be required to keep
records necessary for the determination and ascertainment of the tax in
accordance with the Tax Procedure Act (Cap. 469B).”
50. It was the duty of the Appellant to avail the information and evidence both to the Respondent and to
this Tribunal that would help ascertain whether they opted for an exclusion from the MRI.
51. The Tribunal also notes that the Appellant did not clearly address the dispute around the Input
Tax claims. The Respondent armed that the Appellant made claims on prohibited items as per the
provisions of Section 17(4) of the VAT Act. The Respondent, in their pleadings, mentioned that the
Appellant claimed input tax on car parts and entertainment, which are prohibited as per the Act. This
assertion is unrebutted by the Appellant.
52. The Honorable Tribunal has also previously reiterated the Appellant’s duty to discharge its burden of
proof in the rst instance. In Abyssinia Iron and Steel Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs and Border
Control (TAT No. 435 of 2022), the Tribunal held as follows: -
“The bottom line is that once the Appellant has provided evidence that the Respondent's
assessment was wrong, then the Respondent must push back and show that its assessment
was not arbitrary, capricious, or imagined.” The onus will then shift back to the Appellant
once the Respondent has discharged its burden on a balance of convenience to discharge
the prima facie case that has been presented by the Respondent. “
53. Having considered the pleadings and records before it, the Tribunal is therefore constrained to nd
and hold that the Appellant did not suciently support its objection to the Respondent’s additional
assessment. Therefore, the Respondent was justied in conrming the additional assessment vide its
Objection Decision dated 23rd May, 2025.
Final Disposition
54. Accordingly, the instant appeal fails, and the Tribunal proceeds to make the following orders: -
a. The Appeal be and is hereby dismissed.
b. The Respondent’s Objection Decision dated 23rd May 2025, 2025 be and is hereby upheld.
c. Each party is to bear its own costs.
55. It is so ordered.
DATED AND DELIVERED AT NAIROBI THIS 27THDAY OF MARCH 2026
......................................
DR. RODNEY ODHIAMBO OLUOCH
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 7

CHAIRPERSON
.........................................
CAROLINE WESONGA DR. ERICK KOMOLO
MEMBER MEMBER
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/81/eng@2026-03-27 8
